specification.topic-statement: The external tool executor spawns a child process, passes JSON arguments via stdin, collects stdout as the result, and enforces a 30-second execution timeout.

scope.in-scope: Pipe creation, fork, child process setup (process group isolation, file descriptor redirection, environment variable injection, exec), stdin writing with SIGPIPE protection, stdout and stderr collection into fixed-size buffers, timeout enforcement via alarm(), process wait and exit-code validation, and result allocation.
scope.boundary: The executable path is provided by the caller. This topic does not look up tools by name.
Raw stdout text is returned as-is. JSON success/failure envelope wrapping is applied by the calling layer.

field.name: agent_id
field.note: set as IKIGAI_AGENT_ID environment variable if non-null

field.name: arguments_json
field.note: JSON string written to child's stdin

field.name: child_pid_out
field.note: child PID written here if pointer is non-null

field.name: out_result
field.note: null-terminated stdout content; talloc-owned by caller

behavior.pipe-creation: Three pairs of pipes are created: one for stdin, one for stdout, and one for stderr. If any pipe creation fails, all previously opened file descriptors are closed and an IO error is returned.
behavior.process-fork: The process is forked. If fork fails, all six file descriptors are closed and an IO error is returned.
behavior.child-process-setup:
In the child process: - A new process group is created with the child as leader. This isolates the child from the parent's process group for interrupt handling. - The parent sides of all three pipes are closed. - The child sides are redirected: stdin pipe to fd 0, stdout pipe to fd 1, stderr pipe to fd 2. - If agent_id is non-null, the environment variable IKIGAI_AGENT_ID is set to that value, overwriting any existing value. - The executable at tool_path is executed with no additional arguments (only argv[0] which equals tool_path). - If execution fails, the child exits with status 1 immediately.
All child-process code is excluded from coverage measurement (it runs in a forked copy of the address space, not the test process).
behavior.parent-process: The child's PID is stored in child_pid_out if that pointer is non-null. The child sides of all three pipes are closed. The parent retains the write end of the stdin pipe and the read ends of the stdout and stderr pipes.
behavior.stdin-write: SIGPIPE is temporarily suppressed to prevent a crash if the child exits before reading all input. The arguments JSON string is written to the child's stdin pipe. The write end is closed to signal EOF to the child. The original SIGPIPE disposition is restored.
behavior.output-collection:
A 30-second alarm is set. Stdout is read into a 65,536-byte buffer in a loop until the read returns zero or negative, or the buffer fills to 65,535 bytes. Stderr is read into a separate 65,536-byte buffer in the same manner. The alarm is cancelled after both streams are collected.
If either stdout or stderr exceeds 65,535 bytes, the remainder is silently discarded. No error is reported for truncation.
If the child has not produced output within 30 seconds, the alarm signal interrupts the blocking read call. The read returns -1 and the loop exits. Subsequent processing continues with whatever was collected.
behavior.wait-and-validate:
The child process is waited for. If the child did not exit normally or exited with a non-zero status, an IO error is returned. If stderr content was collected, it is included in the error message; otherwise a generic non-zero-exit message is used.
If the child exited successfully but produced no stdout output, an IO error is returned ("Tool produced no output").
behavior.result-allocation: The stdout buffer is null-terminated. Its contents are copied into a talloc allocation owned by the caller's context. If allocation fails, the process aborts. The pointer is written to out_result and success is returned.
